title: Should sand be treated as a finite resource?
summary: Argue whether the sand that builds our cities should be governed like a scarce, finite resource, given that the world is quietly running short of the one kind of sand concrete actually needs.
topic: Whether sand should be regulated as a scarce resource rather than treated as endless
prompt: Write a 450–600-word argumentative essay on whether sand — the second most-consumed material on earth after water — should be treated and regulated as a finite resource. Take a clear position, give two concrete examples (e.g. river dredging, vanishing beaches), and concede one objection. Use cleft sentences, the passive voice, and complex concession (granted that / even though).
hintStarters: What seems like the most abundant substance on earth turns out, where it actually matters, to be…
Granted that the planet is covered in sand, the cruel detail is that the grains we can build with…
It is not the desert, with all its dunes, but the riverbed and the seabed, that…
Even though no one prices a beach until it has gone, the cost of a missing shoreline is paid by…

hintVocab.meaningVi: một nguồn tài nguyên hữu hạn
sự xây dựng / ngành xây dựng
bổ sung lại / làm đầy lại
việc lấn biển / cải tạo đất
quản trị một tài sản chung

sampleText:
Sand seems like the last thing humanity could ever run out of. It lies in deserts the size of countries; it stretches along every coast; a child digs it up by the bucket. Yet after water, sand is the most consumed material on earth, and the kind we are consuming is quietly disappearing. The argument I want to make is uncomfortable precisely because it sounds absurd: sand should be treated, priced, and governed as a finite resource, before the cheapest building block on the planet becomes a cause of conflict.

The paradox lies in what concrete actually needs. What seems like the most abundant substance on earth turns out, where it actually matters, to be surprisingly rare. Desert sand is useless for building: blown for millennia by the wind, its grains are rounded smooth and will not lock together in concrete. It is not the desert, with all its dunes, but the riverbed and the seabed, that hold the angular, gritty sand that construction demands. Dubai, a city ringed by desert, imports sand from Australia to make its towers. The grains that matter are made slowly, by water grinding rock over thousands of years, and they are being taken out far faster than rivers can replenish them.

Granted that the planet is covered in sand, the cruel detail is that the grains we can build with are a fraction of the whole, and that fraction sits in living systems we are tearing apart to reach. When a riverbed is dredged, the banks slump, the water table drops, and the fish that fed a village go with the sand. Whole sandbars and small islands have been mined out of existence in South and Southeast Asia; in some river deltas the extraction is so heavy that the coast is no longer fed with sediment and the sea is allowed to march inland. Even though no one prices a beach until it has gone, the cost of a missing shoreline is paid by the farmers whose fields turn salty and the towns left without a barrier against storms.

The objection is serious and deserves a fair hearing. Sand is genuinely cheap, heavy, and local; treating it as a precious commodity, critics argue, would raise the price of housing for exactly the poor countries that need to build the most, and a strict licence regime could simply push extraction into a black market run by criminals who answer to no environmental rule at all. This is not a fantasy. Where sand mining has been restricted without enforcement, illegal dredging has flourished and the violence around it has grown. Regulation that exists only on paper can be worse than none.

Even so, the answer to bad regulation is good regulation, not the pretence that the resource is endless. Treating sand as finite need not mean banning it; it means pricing the damage that extraction does, licensing where rivers can bear it, and rewarding the builders who use recycled aggregate or crushed rock instead of virgin grains. It is cheaper to design a tax and a standard now than to fight over a riverbed later. A material is called infinite only until the moment people start killing for it, and along several of the world's rivers that moment has already arrived. The grains beneath our cities were laid down over ten thousand years; the least we owe them is to stop spending them as though tomorrow were guaranteed to send more.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: It-cleft to correct where the useful sand actually is
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'It is not the desert, with all its dunes, but the riverbed and the seabed, that hold the angular, gritty sand …' dùng it-cleft với cặp 'not X, but Y' để sửa lại lầm tưởng rằng sa mạc là nguồn cát xây dựng, chỉ ra rằng cát hữu dụng nằm ở sông và biển.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'It is not the desert, with all its dunes, but the riverbed and the seabed, that hold the angular, gritty sand …' uses an it-cleft with 'not X, but Y' to correct the assumption that deserts supply building sand, pointing instead to rivers and seabeds.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Concession + passive to show damage done without an actor
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'Granted that the planet is covered in sand, …' nhượng bộ bằng 'granted that', còn các câu bị động như 'a riverbed is dredged', 'the sea is allowed to march inland' và 'they are being taken out' đẩy tác nhân ra khỏi câu để nhấn vào hậu quả của việc khai thác.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'Granted that the planet is covered in sand, …' concedes with 'granted that', while passives such as 'a riverbed is dredged', 'the sea is allowed to march inland', and 'they are being taken out' push the agent aside to foreground the consequences of extraction.

sampleTranslationVi:
Cát có vẻ là thứ cuối cùng mà nhân loại có thể cạn kiệt. Nó nằm trong những sa mạc rộng bằng cả một quốc gia; nó trải dài dọc mọi bờ biển; một đứa trẻ đào nó lên từng xô một. Vậy mà sau nước, cát là vật liệu được tiêu thụ nhiều nhất trên trái đất, và loại cát mà ta đang tiêu thụ thì đang âm thầm biến mất. Lập luận tôi muốn đưa ra khó chịu chính vì nó nghe có vẻ phi lý: cát nên được đối xử, định giá và quản trị như một nguồn tài nguyên hữu hạn, trước khi viên gạch xây dựng rẻ nhất hành tinh trở thành một nguyên nhân của xung đột.

Nghịch lý nằm ở chỗ bê tông thực sự cần gì. Cái có vẻ là chất dồi dào nhất trên trái đất hóa ra, ở chỗ nó thực sự quan trọng, lại khan hiếm đến bất ngờ. Cát sa mạc vô dụng cho việc xây dựng: bị gió thổi suốt hàng ngàn năm, các hạt của nó bị mài tròn nhẵn và sẽ không khóa chặt vào nhau trong bê tông. Không phải sa mạc, với tất cả những đụn cát của nó, mà là lòng sông và đáy biển, mới chứa thứ cát sắc cạnh, nhám ráp mà ngành xây dựng cần. Dubai, một thành phố bị sa mạc bao quanh, nhập khẩu cát từ Úc để xây những tòa tháp của mình. Những hạt cát quan trọng được tạo ra một cách chậm chạp, bởi nước nghiền đá qua hàng ngàn năm, và chúng đang bị lấy đi nhanh hơn nhiều so với khả năng các con sông bổ sung lại.

Công nhận rằng hành tinh này phủ đầy cát, chi tiết tàn nhẫn là những hạt mà ta có thể xây dựng được chỉ là một phần nhỏ của tổng thể, và phần nhỏ ấy lại nằm trong những hệ sinh thái sống động mà ta đang xé toạc ra để chạm tới. Khi một lòng sông bị nạo vét, bờ sông sụt xuống, mực nước ngầm hạ thấp, và những con cá nuôi sống một ngôi làng ra đi cùng với cát. Cả những doi cát và những hòn đảo nhỏ đã bị khai thác đến mức biến mất ở Nam Á và Đông Nam Á; ở một số vùng châu thổ, việc khai thác nặng nề đến mức bờ biển không còn được cung cấp phù sa và biển được phép tiến vào đất liền. Dù chẳng ai định giá một bãi biển cho đến khi nó biến mất, cái giá của một đường bờ biển bị mất lại do những người nông dân có ruộng hóa mặn và những thị trấn bị bỏ lại không còn rào chắn trước bão tố gánh chịu.

Phản đối này là nghiêm túc và đáng được lắng nghe công bằng. Cát quả thực rẻ, nặng và sẵn tại chỗ; coi nó như một món hàng quý giá, những người chỉ trích lập luận, sẽ làm tăng giá nhà ở cho đúng những nước nghèo cần xây dựng nhiều nhất, và một chế độ cấp phép nghiêm ngặt có thể đơn giản đẩy việc khai thác vào một thị trường chợ đen do những kẻ tội phạm chẳng tuân theo quy tắc môi trường nào điều hành. Đây không phải là chuyện tưởng tượng. Nơi việc khai thác cát bị hạn chế mà không có sự thực thi, việc nạo vét trái phép lại nở rộ và bạo lực xung quanh nó đã gia tăng. Quy định chỉ tồn tại trên giấy có thể còn tệ hơn là không có quy định.

Dẫu vậy, câu trả lời cho quy định tồi là quy định tốt, chứ không phải sự giả vờ rằng nguồn tài nguyên là vô tận. Coi cát là hữu hạn không nhất thiết có nghĩa là cấm nó; nó có nghĩa là định giá thiệt hại mà việc khai thác gây ra, cấp phép ở những nơi sông có thể chịu được, và tưởng thưởng cho những nhà xây dựng dùng cốt liệu tái chế hay đá nghiền thay vì những hạt cát nguyên sinh. Thiết kế một sắc thuế và một tiêu chuẩn ngay bây giờ thì rẻ hơn là tranh giành một lòng sông về sau. Một vật liệu chỉ được gọi là vô tận cho đến khoảnh khắc người ta bắt đầu giết nhau vì nó, và dọc theo vài con sông của thế giới khoảnh khắc ấy đã đến rồi. Những hạt cát bên dưới các thành phố của chúng ta đã được hình thành qua mười ngàn năm; điều tối thiểu ta nợ chúng là ngừng tiêu xài chúng như thể ngày mai chắc chắn sẽ gửi thêm tới.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses an it-cleft to correct where useful building sand is found?
options: The desert holds the gritty sand construction demands, not the riverbed with its dunes.
Holding the sand is what the desert the riverbed seabed construction does demands.
It is not the desert, with all its dunes, but the riverbed and the seabed, that hold the angular, gritty sand that construction demands.
Construction demands the riverbed seabed dunes desert it is not but that gritty sand hold.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'It is not X, but Y, that …' is an it-cleft correcting the desert assumption.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the 'It is not X, but Y, that …' frame.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence opens with a 'granted that' concession and continues naturally?
options: Granted that the planet is covered in sand, but the cruel detail buildable grains a fraction is whole.
The planet covered in sand granted that, a fraction the grains cruel detail whole the is.
Granted sand covered the planet that in, the whole grains buildable fraction cruel detail a is.
Granted that the planet is covered in sand, the cruel detail is that the buildable grains are a fraction of the whole.
mcQuestions.explanation: The 'Granted that …, …' clause concedes the abundance and then states the catch cleanly.
mcQuestions.hint: A clause starting with 'granted that' should not be followed by 'but'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses the passive to foreground the consequences of dredging?
options: When a riverbed is dredged, the banks slump, the water table drops, and the fish that fed a village go with the sand.
Miners dredge the riverbed and they make the banks slump and water drop low.
Dredging the riverbed is what the banks the water table the fish does village.
The fish village fed go with sand the banks slump water table dredged is when riverbed a.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'When a riverbed is dredged' uses the passive to put the focus on the damage rather than the miner.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option opens with 'is + past participle' and lists the effects?

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'a finite resource' mean as the essay uses it?
options: A resource that refills itself instantly
A resource with limited supply that can run out if overused
A resource that is illegal to sell
A resource found only in deserts
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay argues sand is limited and can be exhausted, so it should be governed accordingly.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of something that has an end.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence concedes the economic objection most fairly?
options: Anyone worried about housing costs is just an enemy of the environment.
Obviously regulation never causes any problems at all.
Sand is genuinely cheap, heavy, and local; treating it as precious would raise housing costs for the poor countries that need to build the most.
Poor countries deserve whatever happens to their rivers.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option B fairly states the cost-and-black-market objection before the essay answers it.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option states the opponents' real concern without insulting them?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option hedges the regulation problem appropriately?
options: All regulation always works perfectly everywhere.
Regulation can never have any downside whatsoever.
Paper regulation black market worse none always perfect works.
Regulation that exists only on paper can be worse than none.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'can be worse than none' concedes that unenforced rules may backfire, hedging the proposal.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the option that admits regulation can go wrong.

mcQuestions.prompt: According to the essay, why is desert sand useless for concrete?
options: Wind has rounded its grains smooth, so they will not lock together
It is too wet to mix
It is too expensive to transport
It contains too much salt
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay says wind-rounded grains are smooth and cannot bind in concrete.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence keeps a consistent argumentative register?
options: Honestly the whole sand thing is super scary and nobody talks about it, right?
The grains that matter are made slowly, by water grinding rock over thousands of years.
Basically we're just running out of sand, simple as that, scary stuff.
It's kind of nuts how, like, Dubai has to import sand, you know?
mcQuestions.explanation: Option B is precise and analytical; the others slip into casual speech.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which transition best pivots from the regulation objection back to the thesis?
options: Anyway, regulation is the only thing that ever matters here.
In conclusion, all regulation should be abandoned immediately.
Even so, the answer to bad regulation is good regulation, not the pretence that the resource is endless.
On the contrary, sand mining has never harmed anyone.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Even so, …' accepts the objection then reasserts the case for better, not absent, governance.
mcQuestions.hint: Which phrase accepts the prior point and reasserts the argument?

options: To refill completely
To regulate by law
To export to another country
To use up or reduce a supply until little remains
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Deplete' means to drain or exhaust a stock of something.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of running a supply down.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses parallel structure most effectively?
options: pricing the damage extraction does, licensing where rivers can bear it, and rewarding builders who use recycled aggregate
pricing the damage, to license where rivers bear it, and you reward builders recycled aggregate
price the damage, the licensing rivers bear, and to reward recycled builders aggregate use
the damage pricing, license where rivers, and rewarded builders aggregate recycled use it
mcQuestions.explanation: Three matched '-ing' phrases form a clean parallel list of policy measures.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option keeps all three verbs in the same '-ing' form?

mcQuestions.prompt: What is the essay's central position?
options: That sand mining should be banned entirely worldwide
That sand should be priced and governed as a finite resource, through better regulation rather than pretending it is endless
That sand is genuinely infinite and needs no oversight
That deserts should be mined instead of rivers
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay argues for treating sand as finite via good regulation, not bans or denial.
mcQuestions.hint: Reread the opening and final paragraphs.

options: To export a resource
To make a resource illegal
To refill or restore a supply that has been used
To grind rock into powder
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Replenish' means to restock; rivers cannot replace sand as fast as it is taken.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of nature filling the supply back up.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option best captures the essay's nuanced conclusion?
options: Sand will always be free and abundant, so nothing needs to change.
Only banning all construction can save the world's rivers.
Importing sand from Australia solves the entire problem.
A material is called infinite only until people start killing for it, and the least we owe these ancient grains is to stop spending them as if tomorrow guarantees more.
mcQuestions.explanation: The conclusion warns the 'infinite' illusion ends in conflict and urges restraint now.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the option about scarcity, conflict, and restraint.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses an em-dash insertion correctly to add a defining aside?
options: Dubai, a city ringed by desert, imports sand from Australia to make its towers.
Dubai imports sand — a city ringed desert — from Australia to make towers its by.
From Australia Dubai — imports sand a city — ringed by desert towers make to its.
A city ringed by desert — Dubai imports — sand to make from Australia its towers.
mcQuestions.explanation: The appositive 'a city ringed by desert' is set off cleanly and the main clause reads in order.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option keeps the city's description as a neat aside?

options: Returning land to its original owners
Creating new land, often by filling shallow sea with sand
Banning construction near rivers
Recycling old building materials
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Land reclamation' is building new ground out of water, a major driver of sand demand.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of making new land where there was sea.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Sand feels endless because we never see it run out in our own lifetime. What other resource in your daily life do you treat as infinite simply because its limit lies beyond your own horizon — and who, somewhere downstream, is already paying for that assumption?
